items.q: If E°cell for a redox reaction is positive, what does that tell you?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — but it may still be very slow if kinetic barriers are high.
items.misconception: A positive E°cell guarantees the reaction will happen rapidly at room temperature.
items.random_wrong: A positive E°cell means the reaction is exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students equate 'feasible' with 'fast', missing the thermodynamics-vs-kinetics distinction (same error as with negative ΔG).

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.
Correct answer: K. “Li” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Limewater turns cloudy in the presence of:
items.why: Correct answer: CO₂. “H₂” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A pure substance melts at exactly 0°C. An impure sample melts at -2°C. What is the depression in melting point?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 °C. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
